content.text: (formerly GPT Index) is a Python framework for building applications that connect LLMs to external data. Its primary use case is

: ingest documents, embed them into a vector store, and answer questions using retrieved context. It also provides primitives for building agents, multi-turn chat engines, and structured query pipelines.
The framework's core strength is abstracting the data pipeline — loading, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieving, and synthesising — so you can focus on application logic rather than plumbing.
The monorepo structure separates integrations into optional packages. Install the
package and add integrations as needed —
object (new in v0.10+) to configure the LLM, embedding model, and chunk sizes. Set it once at application startup.

from llama_index.core import Settings
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

Settings.llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0.1)
Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")
Settings.chunk_size = 512
Settings.chunk_overlap = 64
All indexes, query engines, and chat engines created after this point inherit these settings unless explicitly overridden.
Documents are the starting point of every LlamaIndex pipeline. Use the
for local files, or one of the many loader integrations for databases, APIs, and cloud storage.

from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
print(f"Loaded {len(documents)} documents")
# Each document is a Document object with text and metadata
print(documents[0].text[:200])
print(documents[0].metadata)  # {"file_name": "guide.pdf", "page_label": "1", ...}

from llama_index.core import Document

docs = [
    Document(text="LlamaIndex is a data framework for LLM applications."),
    Document(text="It supports RAG, agents, and structured data extraction."),
]

from llama_index.readers.web import SimpleWebPageReader

documents = SimpleWebPageReader(html_to_text=True).load_data(
    urls=["https://docs.llamaindex.ai"]
)
chunks documents into nodes, embeds them, and stores them in an in-memory vector store. It is the most common index type for RAG.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)
# This call: chunks each document, embeds each chunk, and stores in memory
calls the embedding model once per chunk. For large document sets, persist the index to avoid re-embedding on every startup (see the Persistence section below).
A query engine takes a question, retrieves the most relevant nodes from the index, and synthesises a response using the LLM.

query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What is LlamaIndex used for?")
print(response)                         # the answer string
print(response.source_nodes)            # the retrieved chunks used
for node in response.source_nodes:
    print(node.score, node.text[:100])  # relevance score + chunk preview

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=5,          # retrieve top-5 most similar chunks
    response_mode="tree_summarize",  # summarise across retrieved chunks
)
response = query_engine.query("Summarise the key features of LlamaIndex.")
print(response)
Iteratively refine the answer by passing each chunk to the LLM in sequence (default)
Fit as many chunks as possible in one prompt, then refine if needed (fewer LLM calls)
Build a summary tree bottom-up — good for long documents
Combine all chunks in a single LLM call — fast but may truncate
Return only the retrieved nodes without calling the LLM
A chat engine wraps the query engine with conversation memory. Use it when users need to ask follow-up questions.

chat_engine = index.as_chat_engine(chat_mode="condense_plus_context")

# First turn
response = chat_engine.chat("What is LlamaIndex?")
print(response)

# Follow-up — chat engine resolves "it" using prior context
response = chat_engine.chat("What are its main index types?")
print(response)

# Reset if starting a new conversation
chat_engine.reset()
Condense history into a new query, then retrieve from the index
Retrieve context on every turn; chat history kept separately
Use a ReAct agent loop with the index as a tool
OpenAI function-calling loop; index exposed as a tool
Alias for condense_plus_context; the recommended default
Persist an in-memory index to disk so you do not re-embed documents on every restart.

# Persist after first build
index.storage_context.persist(persist_dir="./index_storage")

# Load on subsequent runs
from llama_index.core import StorageContext, load_index_from_storage

storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(persist_dir="./index_storage")
index = load_index_from_storage(storage_context)
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
A live index can be updated without rebuilding from scratch.

from llama_index.core import Document

# Insert a new document
new_doc = Document(text="LlamaIndex v0.11 adds multi-modal support.")
index.insert(new_doc)

# Delete by document ID
doc_id = documents[0].doc_id
index.delete_ref_doc(doc_id, delete_from_docstore=True)
Attach metadata to documents and filter retrieved nodes at query time to restrict results to a subset of the index.

from llama_index.core import Document, VectorStoreIndex
from llama_index.core.vector_stores import MetadataFilter, MetadataFilters

docs = [
    Document(text="Q1 revenue was $4.2M.", metadata={"quarter": "Q1", "year": 2024}),
    Document(text="Q2 revenue was $5.1M.", metadata={"quarter": "Q2", "year": 2024}),
    Document(text="Q3 revenue was $6.0M.", metadata={"quarter": "Q3", "year": 2024}),
]
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(docs)

filters = MetadataFilters(filters=[
    MetadataFilter(key="quarter", value="Q2"),
])
query_engine = index.as_query_engine(filters=filters)
response = query_engine.query("What was the revenue?")
print(response)   # answers only from Q2 document

Smaller chunks (128–256 tokens) produce more precise retrieval but lose surrounding context. Larger chunks (512–1024 tokens) carry more context but reduce precision. Start with 512 and tune based on your document structure.
after building an index, the persisted index is no longer compatible — embeddings were generated with the old settings. Rebuild from scratch.
Documents generated from the same file path share the same
by default (derived from the file path). If you need to re-ingest a file, explicitly set a new
or delete the old document first.

Node postprocessors filter, re-rank, or transform the retrieved nodes before they are passed to the LLM for synthesis. They sit between the retriever and the response synthesiser in the query pipeline.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import (
    SimilarityPostprocessor,
    KeywordNodePostprocessor,
    SentenceTransformerRerank,
)

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=10,   # retrieve more candidates before filtering
    node_postprocessors=[
        SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.75),   # drop low-score nodes
        KeywordNodePostprocessor(
            required_keywords=["llama"],    # node must contain this keyword
            exclude_keywords=["deprecated"],
        ),
    ],
)

response = query_engine.query("Tell me about LlamaIndex agents.")
A cross-encoder re-ranker re-scores retrieved nodes using a more powerful (but slower) model. Retrieve a large candidate set (top-20) and re-rank to the top-5 for synthesis.

from llama_index.core.postprocessor import SentenceTransformerRerank

# pip install sentence-transformers
reranker = SentenceTransformerRerank(
    model="cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-2-v2",
    top_n=5,
)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=20,
    node_postprocessors=[reranker],
)
provides a composable way to define transformations applied to documents before indexing. Unlike the one-shot
, a pipeline can be cached and rerun incrementally.

from llama_index.core import Document
from llama_index.core.ingestion import IngestionPipeline
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitter
from llama_index.core.extractors import TitleExtractor, KeywordExtractor
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

pipeline = IngestionPipeline(
    transformations=[
        SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64),
        TitleExtractor(nodes=5),          # infer title from first 5 nodes
        KeywordExtractor(keywords=10),    # extract top-10 keywords per node
        OpenAIEmbedding(),                # embed each node
    ]
)

documents = [Document(text="LlamaIndex provides a data framework for LLM apps.")]
nodes = pipeline.run(documents=documents)
print(f"Generated {len(nodes)} nodes")
print(nodes[0].metadata)   # includes title, keywords, and other extracted metadata
Pipelines can also be run asynchronously and with a
that skips already-processed documents — important when re-running on large document sets where only a few files changed.
Using Chroma or Pinecone as the Vector Store
Replace the default in-memory vector store with a persistent, scalable backend for production.

import chromadb
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.vector_stores.chroma import ChromaVectorStore

chroma_client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
chroma_collection = chroma_client.get_or_create_collection("my_docs")

vector_store = ChromaVectorStore(chroma_collection=chroma_collection)
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)

# Build or reload — Chroma persists automatically
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    storage_context=storage_context,
)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What does LlamaIndex support?")

from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec
from llama_index.vector_stores.pinecone import PineconeVectorStore
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext

pc = Pinecone(api_key="YOUR_PINECONE_API_KEY")
pc.create_index(
    name="llamaindex-docs",
    dimension=1536,   # match your embedding model's output dimension
    metric="cosine",
    spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1"),
)
pinecone_index = pc.Index("llamaindex-docs")

vector_store = PineconeVectorStore(pinecone_index=pinecone_index)
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents, storage_context=storage_context)
LlamaIndex ships several node parsers that control how documents are split into chunks. Choosing the right one affects retrieval precision.

from llama_index.core.node_parser import (
    SentenceSplitter,       # split on sentence boundaries, then merge to chunk_size
    TokenTextSplitter,      # split strictly by token count
    MarkdownNodeParser,     # respect Markdown headings as split boundaries
    CodeSplitter,           # split code files by function/class boundaries
    SemanticSplitterNodeParser,  # split at semantic breakpoints (uses embedding similarity)
)
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader, VectorStoreIndex

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./code").load_data()

# CodeSplitter respects function and class boundaries in source code
from llama_index.core.node_parser import CodeSplitter
splitter = CodeSplitter(language="python", chunk_lines=40, chunk_lines_overlap=5)
nodes = splitter.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes)
Enable streaming to start displaying the model's response token-by-token instead of waiting for the full answer.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(streaming=True)
streaming_response = query_engine.query("Explain LlamaIndex in detail.")

# Print tokens as they arrive
streaming_response.print_response_stream()

# Or iterate manually
for token in streaming_response.response_gen:
    print(token, end="", flush=True)
LlamaIndex includes evaluation utilities to measure RAG system quality, helping you identify whether poor answers result from bad retrieval or bad synthesis.

from llama_index.core.evaluation import (
    FaithfulnessEvaluator,   # is the answer grounded in the retrieved context?
    RelevancyEvaluator,      # is the answer relevant to the question?
)

faithfulness_eval = FaithfulnessEvaluator()
relevancy_eval = RelevancyEvaluator()

query = "What is LlamaIndex used for?"
response = query_engine.query(query)

# Faithfulness: does the answer contain information not in the source nodes?
faithfulness_result = await faithfulness_eval.aevaluate_response(response=response)
print(f"Faithful: {faithfulness_result.passing}")

# Relevancy: does the retrieved context match the query?
relevancy_result = await relevancy_eval.aevaluate_response(query=query, response=response)
print(f"Relevant: {relevancy_result.passing}")
All major LlamaIndex components have async equivalents, important for building responsive web APIs.

import asyncio
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine()

async def main():
    # Async query
    response = await query_engine.aquery("What is LlamaIndex?")
    print(response)

    # Async chat
    chat_engine = index.as_chat_engine()
    chat_response = await chat_engine.achat("Tell me about the framework.")
    print(chat_response)

asyncio.run(main())
LlamaIndex connects LLMs to external data through a pipeline: load → chunk → embed → index → retrieve → synthesise
is the global configuration object for LLM, embedding model, and chunking parameters
is the standard starting point — it chunks, embeds, and indexes in one call
on documents enables filtered retrieval — retrieve from a subset of the index at query time
Chunk size is the primary knob for quality tuning — start at 512 tokens and adjust based on document structure
